rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions (the top 8 and bottom 13 of the original ranking); ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank and the published set is a bimodal artifact, not a representative distribution. Delaware does not currently appear in the published index at all and must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt — see rank_note; the published set contains ranks 1-8 and 9-21 of the original ranking only, ranks 9-38 of the true ordering are missing, and is bimodal not representative). Calibrated to methodology anchors and sanity-checked against named published peers (Connecticut 83.0, Idaho 25.0 — the canonical scorer reproduces both exactly from their stored dimension values, confirming the formula matches the one that produced the published set) and against the other five states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Ohio 38.1, Michigan 51.9 (superseded, corrected 53.8), Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9. Delaware is unusually consistent across its eight dimensions (spread of only 1.0 raw points, EQU 3.6 highest to BND 2.6 lowest) but earns no integration premium because the methodology only rewards consistency when it is paired with strength — Delaware is evenly mid-range, not evenly strong. Composite verified with computeCompositeFromDimensions in site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs: dimension mean 3.15, baseComposite 53.75, stdDev 0.2958 (consistencyMult 1.0), 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. Function returns {"composite":53.8,"band":"Functional"} — matches the target exactly.
attribution_note: Scores Delaware's own state conduct. Delaware's role as the incorporation venue for most large US public companies is scored in exactly three places where Delaware exercised its own discretion: S4 (the state does not question, and in 2025 legislatively entrenched, its revenue dependence on corporate domicile), I1 (Senate Bill 21, passed within roughly three months of the December 2024 Chancery ruling against Tesla and the resulting 'DExit' campaign, narrowed shareholder protections under financial pressure), and AB3 (Delaware's separately weak public-records enforcement regime interacts with the corporate-secrecy point). The assessment does not credit Delaware for the Court of Chancery's reputation for judicial quality and does not penalize Delaware for the conduct of companies merely incorporated there — a Delaware-incorporated company's labor practices elsewhere are that state's and that company's conduct, not Delaware's. The 2023 federal Child and Family Services Review found Delaware not in substantial conformity on two permanency outcomes; this is a federal finding, but Delaware's own response (the June 2024 Child and Family Services Plan) is what is scored (A3/BND). Delaware's 2026 budget response to federal Medicaid cuts and its state-level immigration enforcement posture for 2025-2026 could not be researched before the search budget was exhausted and are not reflected in the current scores; this is flagged as an evidence gap, not scored either way.
key_evidence: Delaware is a genuinely middle-performing state: it scores 53.8, has no exemplary dimension and no critical one, and the spread between its best dimension (Equity, 65) and worst (Boundaries, 40) is just 25 points.

Delaware's biggest compassion win came from a lawsuit, not from Delaware itself: the 2020 NAACP Delaware State Conference settlement created a permanent weighted school-funding stream that reached $63 million in FY2025, though an independent 2023 review still found funding short by upward of $1 billion.

One third of Delaware's operating budget depends on being the easiest place in America to incorporate — roughly $2.1 billion projected to the general fund by mid-2026 from corporate franchise revenue — and after the December 2024 Chancery ruling against Tesla, Senate Bill 21 passed the Senate 20-0 and the House 32-7 within about three months, narrowing shareholder protections; Delaware moved faster on corporate revenue than on any social policy in five years.

Confirmed drug deaths fell 36% in one year, from 527 in 2023 to 338 in 2024, after the state targeted naloxone distribution to the highest-risk zip codes — the clearest case of Delaware's own action reducing suffering.

In January 2025 Delaware counted 1,585 people without homes, a 16% year-over-year rise, with children under 18 making up 27.3% of them, against about 1,200 emergency shelter beds and fewer than 400 transitional beds statewide — the state measures this problem well and cannot yet meet it.
